1: A theme: "In you, LORD, I take refuge; let me never be put to shame (avushah); rescue me by your righteousness." The covenant refuge-and-righteousness structure — taking shelter in YHWH and being rescued by his tzedakah (righteous deliverance) — is the OT template for the NT's doctrine of justification. Romans 1:17 ("in the gospel the righteousness of God is revealed") and Philippians 3:9 ("not having a righteousness of my own... but that which is through faith in Christ") develop the "rescued by your righteousness" of Psalms 31:1 to its fulfillment. "Let me never be put to shame" — the covenant promise that faith in YHWH is not ultimately disappointed — is what Paul says of Christ: "anyone who trusts in him will never be put to shame" (Romans 10:11, citing Isaiah 28:16).
2: A theme: "Turn your ear to me; quickly rescue me. Be my rock of refuge, a strong fortress to save me." The petition for a "rock of refuge" continues the rock-theology of Psalms 28:1 ("my Rock"). 1 Corinthians 10:4 identifies the accompanying rock as Christ; the rock-fortress that the psalmist petitions YHWH to be is the salvation that Christ provides. Matthew 7:24-25 (building on the rock that does not fall) applies rock-foundation imagery to obedience to Christ's words — the same "rock of refuge" language, with Christ as both the rock and the one who points toward it.
3: A theme: "For you are my rock and my fortress; lead me and guide me for your name's sake." "For your name's sake" — the divine motivation for guiding and rescuing is the divine reputation: the name of YHWH is at stake in the fate of his servant. The same logic applies in the NT to Christ's work: Ephesians 1:12-14 ("for the praise of his glory"); Philippians 2:9-11 ("the name that is above every name... to the glory of God the Father"). The guidance "for the name's sake" becomes in the NT the whole economy of salvation, conducted "to the praise of his glorious grace" (Ephesians 1:6).
4: A theme: "Free me from the trap they have secretly set for me, for you are my refuge." The hidden trap — the secret plot against the righteous sufferer — is the pattern that the passion narratives develop in their accounts of the secret plots against Jesus (Mark 14:1: "the chief priests and the teachers of the law were scheming to arrest Jesus secretly and kill him"; Luke 22:2: "they were afraid of the people"). Psalms 31:4's prayer for freedom from the secretly set trap is the prayer that the psalm's trajectory will ultimately answer through resurrection: the trap was sprung (the arrest, trial, death) but did not hold, because "the LORD is the refuge" whose deliverance cannot be permanently thwarted.
5: A direct citation: "Into your hand I commit my spirit (beyad-kha afkid ruhi); you have redeemed me, LORD, faithful God." Luke 23:46 — Jesus's final words from the cross — are an explicit quotation of this verse: "Jesus called out with a loud voice, 'Father, into your hands I commit my spirit.' When he had said this, he breathed his last." Christ took the psalmist's prayer of trust in the moment of mortal crisis and made it his own dying breath. The original prayer is a statement of confidence in YHWH's faithfulness even in death; Jesus transforms it into the ultimate act of filial trust — the Son committing his spirit into the Father's hand. The addition of "Father" to the quotation deepens the Sonship dimension: what was a covenant servant's prayer becomes the Son's surrender to the Father.
6: A theme: "I despise those who cling to worthless idols; but I trust in the LORD." The double movement — repudiating false trust and affirming true trust — is the structure of the covenant's exclusive loyalty. Jonah 2:8 echoes this ("those who cling to worthless idols forfeit the grace that could be theirs"), suggesting the phrase was formulaic covenant language. Christ's exclusive loyalty to the Father throughout his ministry — "I always do what pleases him" (John 8:29) — is the fullest embodiment of this covenant faithfulness. The "trust in the LORD" that the psalmist affirms against the background of worthless alternatives is what Christ models completely in the Gethsemane prayer: "not as I will, but as you will" (Matthew 26:39).
7: A theme: "I will be glad and rejoice in your steadfast love (hesed), for you have seen my distress and known the anguish of my soul." The divine seeing and knowing of the sufferer's distress — the intimate divine knowledge that precedes rescue — is the theological claim that the incarnation fulfills in its deepest form. Hebrews 4:15: "we do not have a high priest who is unable to empathize with our weaknesses, but we have one who has been tempted in every way, just as we are." Christ's incarnation is the ultimate divine "knowing the anguish of the soul" — not from outside but from within human experience. The gladness in covenant love that the psalmist anticipates upon rescue is the joy that "was set before him" that enabled Christ to endure the cross (Hebrews 12:2).
8: A theme: "you did not hand me over to the enemy; you set my feet in a wide-open place." The "wide-open place" (merahav, spacious place, room, relief from constriction) — the rescue from the narrow passage of danger into open space — is the salvation image that Paul uses in 2 Corinthians 6:11-12 (the "open heart") and that the whole NT uses for the freedom of the gospel (Galatians 5:1: "It is for freedom that Christ has set us free"). The constriction of death, law, and sin from which the psalmist is delivered anticipates the comprehensive liberation that Christ's resurrection accomplishes: "where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is freedom" (2 Corinthians 3:17).
9: A theme: "Have mercy on me, LORD, for I am in trouble; my eye wastes with grief — my soul and my body too." The total somatic experience of suffering — eye, soul, and body all wasting together — is the holistic human crisis that requires holistic divine rescue. The incarnation is the divine response to holistic human suffering: Christ took on body, soul, and spirit (not merely a human appearance) precisely in order to rescue the whole person. Hebrews 2:17-18 makes the logic explicit: "he had to be made like them, fully human in every way... because he himself suffered when he was tempted, he is able to help those who are being tempted." The suffering body-and-soul of Psalms 31:9 is the suffering that the incarnate Son entered in order to redeem.
10: A theme: "For my life is exhausted by grief and my years by sighing; my strength fails because of my iniquity, and my bones waste away." The connection of physical exhaustion to moral failure ("my strength fails because of my iniquity") is the OT's recognition that sin and suffering are connected — not as simple cause-and-effect (Job's friends' error) but as covenantal reality (the whole-person disruption that sin causes). Isaiah 53:3-4 uses the same imagery for the Servant: "a man of suffering... he took up our pain and bore our suffering." Christ bore not only the judicial consequence of iniquity (Romans 3:25) but its physical exhaustion (John 19:28: "knowing that everything had now been finished... Jesus said, 'I am thirsty'").
11: A theme: "Among all my enemies I am an object of scorn, and especially to my neighbors; those who know me are afraid — when people see me in the street, they flee from me." The social abandonment and mockery of the righteous sufferer — scorned by enemies, fled from by acquaintances — is the passion narrative in embryo. Mark 14:50: "then everyone deserted him and fled"; Matthew 27:39-44: the passers-by, the chief priests, and those crucified with him all mocked. The "object of scorn" (herpah) of Psalms 31:11 is the same word used in Psalms 69:7, 9, 10, 19, 20 — all applied to Christ in the NT (Romans 15:3; John 2:17; Romans 15:3).
12: A theme: "I am as forgotten as a dead man; I have become like a shattered clay vessel." The image of being as forgotten as a dead man — the socially disappeared, the one whose name is no longer spoken — is the nadir of honor-shame experience. The "shattered clay vessel" (keli oved) is the discarded pot, broken and thrown away. The passion narrative's extreme: Jesus died in disgrace, was buried in another man's tomb, and could have been forgotten. But Isaiah 53:12 says "he poured out his life unto death" in a way that resulted in his receiving "a portion among the great" — the shattered vessel is raised to glory (Philippians 2:8-9). The "forgotten dead man" who is raised becomes the one of whom it is said "he ever lives to intercede" (Hebrews 7:25).
13: A theme: "I hear the rumors of the many — terror surrounds me — as they plot together against me, conspiring to take my life." The conspiracy against the life of the righteous sufferer is the passion narrative's political-religious structure: the "rumors of the many" (the crowd calling for crucifixion), the plots of the Sanhedrin (John 11:53: "from that day on they plotted to take his life"), and the terror of being surrounded. Psalms 31:13 is part of the complex of Passion Psalms (Pss 22, 31, 69) that the Gospel writers drew on to interpret and narrate the events of Good Friday as the fulfillment of the OT pattern of the righteous sufferer.
14: A theme: "But I trusted in you, LORD; I said, 'You are my God.'" The "but" that pivots from the surrounding terror (v13) to the confession of trust is the theological hinge of the entire psalm — and of the passion narrative. Luke 23:46 (Christ's dying words from Psalms 31:5) comes immediately after the darkness and the tearing of the temple veil, but the dying breath is an act of trust. The pivot from surrounding hostility to covenant trust ("you are my God") is what distinguished Christ's death from the deaths of those crucified beside him: in the same circumstances of abandonment and humiliation, Christ confessed the Father.
15: A theme: "My times are in your hand; rescue me from my enemies and from those who pursue me." "My times are in your hand" — the divine sovereignty over the timing and circumstances of the sufferer's life — is the theological claim that the passion narrative embodies. John 7:30 ("no one laid a hand on him, because his hour had not yet come"); John 12:23 ("the hour has come for the Son of Man to be glorified"); John 17:1 ("Father, the hour has come"). Christ's times were in the Father's hand: the cross happened at the appointed time (Galatians 4:4: "when the set time had fully come, God sent his Son"), and the resurrection was timed to the third day as Jesus himself repeatedly announced.
16: A theme: "Make your face shine on your servant; save me through your steadfast love (hesed)." The priestly blessing of Numbers 6:24-26 ("the LORD make his face shine on you and be gracious to you") is the covenantal blessing invoked here in personal petition. Salvation "through steadfast love" is the covenant-mercy basis of all divine rescue — not earned by the servant but grounded in YHWH's committed covenant loyalty. The NT's development: "God demonstrates his own love for us in this: while we were still sinners, Christ died for us" (Romans 5:8). The face that shines in rescue is the face of God revealed in Christ (2 Corinthians 4:6: "the light of the knowledge of God's glory displayed in the face of Christ").
17: A theme: "LORD, let me not be put to shame, for I have called on you; let the wicked be ashamed — let them go silently down to Sheol." The double shame petition — for the righteous not to be shamed and for the wicked to receive shame — is the covenant moral order enforced eschatologically. Romans 10:11 ("anyone who trusts in him will never be put to shame") applies this promise directly to those who trust in Christ. The "not put to shame" is the resurrection vindication: the crucified one who seemed to be shamed publicly (Hebrews 12:2: "enduring the cross, scorning its shame") is raised by the Father, vindicating him and demonstrating that trust in him was not misplaced.
18: A theme: "Let the lying lips be struck silent — lips that speak against the righteous with arrogance and contempt." The false witnesses and false accusations that surrounded Jesus's trial (Mark 14:56-59: "many bore false witness against him") are the literal fulfillment of the "lying lips that speak against the righteous." The prayer that they be struck silent is answered not in the trial itself (where they succeeded) but in the resurrection, which silenced the accusation finally: "who will bring any charge against those whom God has chosen? It is God who justifies" (Romans 8:33). The lying lips of the trial are answered by the divine verdict of the resurrection.
19: A theme: "How great is your goodness, which you have stored up for those who fear you and displayed for those who take shelter in you, in full view of all!" The "stored up" goodness — the eschatological reserve of divine blessing prepared for the covenant-faithful — is what Paul cites in 1 Corinthians 2:9 ("no eye has seen, no ear has heard, no mind has conceived what God has prepared for those who love him"). The goodness that the psalmist expects to be "displayed in full view of all" is the eschatological manifestation of the kingdom: "When Christ, who is your life, appears, then you also will appear with him in glory" (Colossians 3:4).
20: A theme: "In the shelter of your presence you hide them from human plots; you conceal them in your tent from the quarreling of tongues." The divine shelter that hides the vulnerable from human schemes — the protective hiddenness in the divine presence — is the pattern that the NT expresses as being "hidden with Christ in God" (Colossians 3:3). The ultimate shelter is Christ himself: those who are in Christ are protected by the same divine presence that protects the covenant people in Psalms 31:20. Revelation 7:15 ("he who sits on the throne will shelter them with his presence") uses skene (tent/tabernacle), the same sheltering image, for the eschatological refuge of the redeemed.
21: A theme: "Praise the LORD, for he displayed his wonderful steadfast love to me when I was like a man in a besieged city." The "besieged city" image — the surrounded, enclosed, cut off — is the experience of utter isolation and helplessness from which YHWH's hesed rescues. The crucifixion created exactly this besieged-city condition for Jesus: surrounded by enemies, abandoned by friends, cut off. The praise that follows rescue from the besieged city is the Easter praise: "Christ has indeed been raised from the dead" (1 Corinthians 15:20). The hesed that is wonderful (niflat hesdo, his love working wonders/miracles) is the love that performs the miracle of resurrection.
22: A theme: "I said in my panic, 'I am cut off from your sight.' Yet you heard my cries for mercy when I called to you." The experience of being "cut off from sight" — the complete loss of the sense of divine presence — is the subjective experience of abandonment that the psalmist confesses was not the reality. The gap between felt abandonment and actual divine faithfulness is the space within which faith operates — and the space that the resurrection closes. Christ's cry on the cross ("my God, my God, why have you forsaken me?") gives voice to the felt abandonment of Psalms 31:22; the resurrection is the answer that "yet you heard my cries for mercy" — the cry was not unheard, and the Forsaken One was not finally forsaken.
23: A theme: "Love the LORD, all you his faithful people! The LORD protects the faithful and pays back the proud in full." The summons to love YHWH grounded in the covenant confidence that faithfulness is protected and pride repaid is the ethical conclusion of the individual lament. The NT grounding for the same summons: 1 John 4:19 ("we love because he first loved us") — covenant love precedes and enables responsive love. "The LORD protects the faithful" becomes in the NT "I give them eternal life, and they shall never perish; no one will snatch them out of my hand" (John 10:28). Christ is both the one who models the faithfulness of Psalms 31:1-22 and the one through whom the faithful are protected.
24: A theme: "Be strong, and let your heart be courageous, all you who hope in the LORD!" The closing call to courage grounded in hope is the community's appropriate response to everything the psalm has described: the suffering, the deliverance, the vindication. Hebrews 12:1-3 makes the same call from the same foundation: "run with perseverance the race marked out for us, fixing our eyes on Jesus... so that you will not grow weary and lose heart." The courage the psalmist summons is exactly the courage that "fixing our eyes on Jesus" enables — the resurrection confidence that makes present suffering bearable. The hope in YHWH of Psalms 31:24 is the hope centered on Christ that "does not put us to shame, because God's love has been poured out into our hearts" (Romans 5:5).
